dialogue_tree.title: Module 1: Simple Reflections - Building Engagement
dialogue_tree.learning_objective: Learn to use simple reflections to demonstrate understanding and build rapport with a patient in precontemplation
dialogue_tree.description: Simple reflections repeat or rephrase what the patient said to show you're listening and understand. They build trust and encourage the patient to continue exploring.

nodes.patient_statement: My doctor sent me here to talk about quitting smoking, but honestly, I don't see what the big deal is. I've been smoking for 20 years and I'm fine.
nodes.patient_context: Precontemplation - Patient doesn't see smoking as a problem and may feel coerced into the conversation

practitioner_choices.text: Your doctor is concerned about your smoking.
practitioner_choices.feedback: This reflects only part of what the patient said - you missed their perspective that they don't see it as a problem. A more complete reflection would acknowledge their view.

practitioner_choices.text: You don't see your smoking as a problem right now.
practitioner_choices.feedback: Excellent! This simple reflection captures the patient's perspective without judgment. It shows you heard them and creates space for them to continue.

practitioner_choices.text: Even though you've been smoking for 20 years, it's actually causing damage you can't see yet.
practitioner_choices.feedback: This contradicts the patient and jumps to educating them. The 'righting reflex' often increases resistance in precontemplation. Try reflecting their perspective first.

nodes.patient_statement: Yeah, she is. But she worries about everything. I feel fine, my breathing is okay. I don't get what everyone's so worried about.

practitioner_choices.text: From your perspective, everything feels fine with your health.
practitioner_choices.feedback: Good reflection! You're continuing to show understanding of their perspective, which builds rapport even when they're resistant.

practitioner_choices.text: Your breathing feels okay to you right now.
practitioner_choices.feedback: Excellent! This reflects a specific detail the patient mentioned, showing you're listening carefully. Specificity strengthens the reflection.

practitioner_choices.text: But are you sure your breathing is really okay? Sometimes people don't notice gradual changes.
practitioner_choices.feedback: This challenges the patient's perception and can increase defensiveness. In precontemplation, reflecting their view builds more rapport than questioning it.

nodes.patient_statement: Exactly! I mean, if I had a problem, I'd know it, right? I can still do everything I used to do.
nodes.patient_context: Patient feels validated and continues to share their perspective

practitioner_choices.text: You feel like you'd notice if smoking was affecting you.
practitioner_choices.feedback: Perfect! This reflection captures their reasoning and continues to build understanding. You're resisting the urge to correct or educate.

practitioner_choices.text: You can still do everything you used to do.
practitioner_choices.feedback: Good! This is a very literal reflection. While accurate, the previous option added slightly more depth by capturing the underlying belief.

practitioner_choices.text: Actually, many smokers don't realize they're being affected until serious damage has occurred.
practitioner_choices.feedback: This is the 'righting reflex' - jumping to educate. While factually correct, it breaks rapport and increases resistance. Stay with reflections in early engagement.

nodes.patient_statement: Look, I appreciate you trying to help, but I didn't come here to be lectured. I get enough of that from my family.
nodes.patient_context: Patient is becoming resistant due to feeling lectured

practitioner_choices.text: You're feeling lectured, and that's frustrating for you.
practitioner_choices.feedback: Excellent recovery! Reflecting their emotion (frustration) shows empathy and can help repair the rapport. This is 'rolling with resistance.'

practitioner_choices.text: I'm not here to lecture you. I genuinely want to help you be healthier.
practitioner_choices.feedback: Defending yourself can make things worse. Instead, reflect the patient's experience to show you understand their frustration.

nodes.patient_statement: Right. I mean, sure, I cough in the morning sometimes, but that's normal for smokers, isn't it?
nodes.patient_context: Patient mentions a symptom but normalizes it

practitioner_choices.text: You notice some coughing, and you see that as just part of being a smoker.
practitioner_choices.feedback: Excellent! This reflects both parts - the symptom and their interpretation - without judgment. You're building a complete picture of their perspective.

practitioner_choices.text: You cough in the mornings.
practitioner_choices.feedback: This is accurate but misses the patient's interpretation (that they see it as normal). The more complete reflection captures their full meaning.

practitioner_choices.text: That morning cough is actually called 'smoker's cough' and it means your lungs are trying to clear out tar and toxins.
practitioner_choices.feedback: While educational, this breaks the engagement process. You're still building rapport - save education for later when they're more open to it.

nodes.patient_statement: You know what, I don't think this is for me. Thanks anyway.
nodes.patient_context: Patient is disengaging due to feeling pressured or challenged
nodes.outcome: Poor engagement - Patient has withdrawn from the conversation
nodes.learning_summary: When we challenge, educate, or contradict a patient in precontemplation, we often increase resistance rather than build motivation. Simple reflections that demonstrate understanding - even when we disagree - build the foundation for future change conversations.

nodes.patient_statement: Yeah. I walk my dog every day, I work a physical job. If smoking was really hurting me, I couldn't do those things.
nodes.patient_context: Patient provides evidence for their view

practitioner_choices.text: Your daily activities - walking your dog, your physical job - those are evidence to you that you're doing okay.
practitioner_choices.feedback: Perfect! This reflection amplifies what they said, showing you really understand their reasoning. This builds strong rapport.

practitioner_choices.text: You work a physical job.
practitioner_choices.feedback: Accurate but incomplete. You captured one detail but missed the connection they're making between their activities and their health assessment.

nodes.patient_statement: Yeah, it is. Everyone acts like they know what's best for me better than I do.
nodes.patient_context: Patient feels their autonomy is being violated

practitioner_choices.text: You feel like people aren't respecting that you know yourself best.
practitioner_choices.feedback: Excellent! This reflection captures the deeper meaning - their need for autonomy and respect. This kind of reflection deepens rapport.

practitioner_choices.text: People are telling you what to do.
practitioner_choices.feedback: Accurate but stays at the surface. The previous option captures the emotional impact - feeling disrespected - which shows deeper empathy.

nodes.patient_statement: Exactly. My dad smoked till he was 75 and lived a good life. I figure genetics matter more than people think.
nodes.patient_context: Patient shares family history as evidence

practitioner_choices.text: Your dad's experience - smoking and still living to 75 - that shapes how you think about risk.
practitioner_choices.feedback: Excellent! This reflection shows you understand where their beliefs come from. You've built solid rapport through consistent reflection.

practitioner_choices.text: You think genetics are more important than smoking.
practitioner_choices.feedback: Good reflection. Both options are MI-adherent; the first adds slightly more context about why they believe this.

nodes.patient_statement: Right. So when my doctor gets all serious about it, it's hard to take seriously, you know?
nodes.patient_context: Patient feels there's a disconnect between medical advice and their lived experience

practitioner_choices.text: There's a gap between what your doctor is saying and what you're experiencing in your body.
practitioner_choices.feedback: Perfect! This reflection identifies the discrepancy they're experiencing. You've demonstrated deep understanding throughout this conversation.

practitioner_choices.text: It's hard to take your doctor seriously about this.
practitioner_choices.feedback: Good reflection! Both options work well here - the first adds a bit more depth about why it's hard to take seriously.

nodes.patient_statement: Thanks for understanding that. I guess I'm just tired of feeling judged, you know?
nodes.patient_context: Patient appreciates feeling heard and understood

practitioner_choices.text: You're tired of feeling judged about your smoking.
practitioner_choices.feedback: Excellent! By consistently reflecting without judgment, you've repaired the relationship and created an opening for future conversations.

practitioner_choices.text: I understand. So what do you want to do about your smoking?
practitioner_choices.feedback: While this seems reasonable, jumping to action right after they express relief at not being judged can feel like pressure. A simple reflection honors their feeling first.

nodes.patient_statement: Yeah. Look, I appreciate you actually listening instead of just telling me what to do. Maybe we can talk again sometime.
nodes.outcome: Good engagement - Through consistent simple reflections, you built rapport with a resistant patient. They're open to future conversations.
nodes.learning_summary: Simple reflections are the foundation of MI, especially in precontemplation. They demonstrate understanding, build trust, and avoid triggering resistance. By consistently reflecting the patient's perspective - even when you disagree - you create a relationship where change becomes possible in the future.

nodes.patient_statement: Right. Well, I think I'm done here. Have a good day.
nodes.outcome: Engagement failed - Patient withdrew due to feeling pressured or misunderstood
nodes.learning_summary: In precontemplation, the 'righting reflex' - our urge to fix, educate, or convince - often backfires. Simple reflections that honor the patient's current perspective build the foundation for change, even when we strongly disagree with their view.
